principles.paraphrase_en: A court cannot rely on documents or pleadings that have not been properly exhibited as evidence in the proceedings.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of unregistered instruments for locus standi
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether a person has standing to bring an application, the court may consider matters related to instruments that are pending registration.
If a transaction is found to be a sham, it is legally ineffective and cannot convey any interest in property.

principles.principle_label: Summary determination inappropriate with triable issue
principles.paraphrase_en: An originating summons should not be determined summarily if there is at least one triable issue that requires a full trial.

principles.principle_label: Material factual error as basis for appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: A material error of fact that forms a crucial basis for a judge's finding can constitute a valid ground for appeal.

principles.principle_label: Abuse of process in repeated applications
principles.paraphrase_en: A party cannot raise a point in a later application if it was available but not pursued in an earlier interlocutory application, unless there has been a material change in circumstances or new facts.

The principle preventing the raising of points in later applications applies not only where a court has already determined an earlier application, but also where there has been no prior determination by the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Chanel Ltd v FW Woolworth & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the principle first established in Chanel regarding abuse of process when raising points in subsequent applications.

relationships.target_case_name: Gee
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from Gee that the principle is founded on the responsibility to take a point when first reasonably available.

relationships.target_case_name: Compania Sud Americana De Vapores SA
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the broad application of the Chanel principle beyond situations with prior court determinations.

candidates.case_name: AU DIN HAR ELLEN v. AU YEE WAN also known as YEE WAN AU AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must assess the facts and parties' conduct objectively, not based on their unproven subjective state of mind or misconceptions of law.

candidates.case_name: LAI CHIK KUN MICHAEL AND OTHERS v. THE BAPTIST CONVENTION OF HONG KONG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Distinction between civil and criminal contempt
principles.paraphrase_en: The classification of contempt as civil or criminal depends on the nature of the contumelious act. Failure to comply with a court order in civil proceedings constitutes civil contempt, while acts that interfere with the course of justice constitute criminal contempt.

For the purpose of establishing conspiracy, a deliberate decision not to inquire into matters that are obvious and specific can be treated as equivalent to actual knowledge. This principle applies where a person shuts their eyes to an obvious means of knowledge.
A lawyer is generally entitled to rely on the correctness of their client's instructions, unless there is a deliberate avoidance of obvious inquiries that would reveal the truth.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Chu Kong
relationships.description: The judgment applies the distinction between civil and criminal contempt as articulated in Re Chu Kong.

relationships.target_case_name: De Krassel v Chu Vincent
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles regarding blind-eye knowledge and its equivalence to actual knowledge for conspiracy purposes from De Krassel.

candidates.case_name: ROBIN, BERNARDO JR FELIZARTA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement, as established in Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol, prohibits the return of a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.
cited_statutes: Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees
1967 Protocol relating to the Status of Refugees

relationships.target_case_name: Re: Kartini
relationships.description: The present judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini.

principles.principle_label: Reservation of rights in fee disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: A client who has a dispute over legal fees may settle the disputed bills first while expressly reserving the right to refer the bills for taxation, and subsequently commence taxation proceedings to challenge the fees.

relationships.target_case_name: Springbok Shipping (HK) Ltd & others v Clayton Wong & Co
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal authority to support the proposition that paying bills under a reservation of rights preserves the right to taxation.

judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For an interim payment order, the court must first be satisfied that if the case proceeds to trial, the applicant would obtain judgment for a substantial sum of money.

When considering an application for interim payment, the court should read Order 29 rules 11 and 12 together and ask a single question about whether the applicant fulfills the requirements of those rules as a whole, rather than examining each rule separately and exclusively.

relationships.target_case_name: Shearson Lehman Brothers Inc v. Maclaine, Watson & Co. Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the approach from Shearson Lehman Brothers regarding the interpretation of Order 29 rules 11 and 12 for interim payment applications.

judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge F. Li in Chambers (By paper disposal)
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Grounds for leave to appeal - 'some other reason'
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'some other reason' ground for granting leave to appeal is not limited to an exhaustive list of circumstances; it can be invoked when the Court of Appeal finds it in the interests of justice, such as when the case involves a question of general principle being decided for the first time, or a question of importance where further appellate consideration would benefit the public.
The legal test for admitting new evidence after trial for the purpose of an appeal is governed by established authority, which would be applied if a formal application is made.

relationships.target_case_name: Ladd v Marshall
relationships.description: The judgment cites Ladd v Marshall as the well-established authority governing the admissibility of new evidence on appeal.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Thelma Kwan in Chambers (not open to public)
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: The test for apparent bias requires the court to first identify all relevant circumstances and then determine whether a fair-minded and informed observer would conclude there was a real possibility of bias.

principles.principle_label: Court's duty to consider evidence presented
principles.paraphrase_en: The court is not required to search through all trial documents for evidence that parties rely on; parties must direct the court to the specific evidence in their submissions.
The child's best interests are the paramount consideration, as established in guardianship legislation.

principles.principle_label: Admission of new evidence on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: New evidence introduced without court leave on appeal may not be addressed, especially if it does not materially affect the established factual findings.

relationships.target_case_name: GM-SA aka DG, M-S v DDPJ (Recusal)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the established test for apparent bias from this case.

relationships.target_case_name: ZFY v SCHC
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes this case by noting it did not involve arguments about the child's habitual residence in Hong Kong.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: BKGGB also known as NGGB v. BKND
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Thelma Kwan in Chambers (not open to public)
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Ladd v Marshall test for new evidence on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: To admit new evidence on appeal, an applicant must satisfy all three limbs of the Ladd v Marshall test: (1) the evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence for use at trial; (2) the evidence would have an important influence on the result; and (3) the evidence is apparently credible.

principles.principle_label: Bar on raising new points on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: A party who omits to take a point at trial is generally barred from raising it on appeal, unless there is no reasonable possibility that the evidence relevant to that point would have been materially more favourable to the other side had the point been taken at trial.
The principle from Flywin, which restricts raising new points on appeal, has been adopted and applied by the Hong Kong Court of Appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Ladd v Marshall
relationships.description: The judgment applies the three-limb test from Ladd v Marshall to assess an application to admit new evidence on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Flywin
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in Flywin regarding the bar on raising new points on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Mimi Kar Yee Wong Hung v Raymond Kin Sang Hung
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal decision as having adopted the Flywin principle.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: GM SA aka DG, M S v. DDPJ
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge G. Own (By Paper Disposal)
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must identify all relevant circumstances and then assess whether a fair-minded and informed observer would conclude there is a real possibility or danger of tribunal bias.

principles.principle_label: Proper procedure for urgent summons hearings
principles.paraphrase_en: A party seeking an urgent hearing for a summons should first write to the court with full reasons before filing the summons, or alternatively seek an adjournment for longer duration at the first call-over hearing.

principles.principle_label: Application of apparent bias test to recusal
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering recusal, the court must determine whether an objective, fair-minded, and well-informed observer, having considered all facts and circumstances, would conclude there is a real possibility the judge would not be impartial or has prejudged issues.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: CHUNG LOI KIU by her next friend, LAU CHING MAN v. LAU YIP HUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Damian Wong in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Hearsay evidence and interests of justice
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering whether to exclude hearsay evidence, the court must assess if its exclusion would be prejudicial to the interests of justice. This assessment can be informed by considering whether, under the factors in section 49 of the relevant ordinance, the evidence would ultimately carry any weight at trial. If no weight could be attached to it, its exclusion is not prejudicial.

It is a common and accepted practice in legal proceedings for solicitors to prepare witness statements based on instructions from witnesses. The stylistic presentation of the evidence, including word choice and formatting, is typically determined by the solicitor, who is better placed to assess the relevance and weight of different matters.

The court should not automatically dismiss witness statements that are alleged to be problematic or colluded. The correct approach is to examine the evidence carefully rather than rejecting it outright.

principles.principle_label: Adverse possession requires single and exclusive possession
principles.paraphrase_en: The fundamental principle for establishing adverse possession is that the possession must be single and exclusive. There is no fixed or absolute rule for what constitutes such possession; it is a question of fact to be determined on the circumstances of each individual case.

principles.principle_label: Acts of possession as a question of fact and degree
principles.paraphrase_en: An act such as planting trees is one form of using land. Whether this act alone constitutes adverse possession depends on its degree and extent, just as with any other use of land. It is not automatically sufficient or insufficient without examining the specific factual context.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Chung Yee v Chan Wah Cho
relationships.description: The judgment adopts the approach from this case of not automatically dismissing allegedly problematic witness statements.

relationships.target_case_name: Integrity Financial Advice Network Company Limited v Chu Yau Shun
relationships.description: The judgment follows the approach from this case of carefully examining rather than automatically rejecting witness statements with alleged issues.

relationships.target_case_name: Gotland Enterprises Limited v Kwok Chi Yau
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the fundamental principle that adverse possession requires single and exclusive possession.

relationships.target_case_name: The personal representative of the estate of Chan Chuen v Forestside Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as an example where the court examined further steps taken by a squatter beyond equivocal tree planting.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Pui Wan v Wong Wing Kwong
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as an authority where the court looked for additional exclusionary acts beyond mere tree planting.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Tin Bo v The estate of Ng Cheong
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as an authority where the court examined what further steps were taken by a squatter to exclude others.
